Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high level with income, then expenses, then what you’ve got saved, and we’ll hit any insurance and debts along the way. David, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, then we’ll tighten it up.
Client 1: Roughly one-eighty a year, give or take.
Advisor: Okay—when you say one-eighty, do you mean around $180,000, or do you have a more exact figure you use for planning?
Client 1: The number we’ve been using is 181100 annually—household gross.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—David, your self-employed income is part of that, and Jennifer, you’ve got income coming in too, right?

Client 2: It’s 44750 a year, and it comes monthly.
Advisor: Perfect. Switching to spending for a second—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in?
Client 2: We’re pretty steady—about 5400 a month.
Advisor: Okay. And debt payments—mortgage, car loans, credit cards—what’s the total you’re paying monthly toward debt right now?
Client 1: Zero. No monthly debt payments—0.

Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high level with income, then expenses, then what you’ve got saved, and we’ll hit any insurance and debts along the way. David, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, then we’ll tighten it up.
Client 1: Roughly one-eighty a year, give or take.
Advisor: Okay—when you say one-eighty, do you mean around $180,000, or do you have a more exact figure you use for planning?
Client 1: The number we’ve been using is 181100 annually—household gross.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—David, your self-employed income is part of that, and Jennifer, you’ve got income coming in too, right?

Client 2: Right, we don’t have any loans right now.
Advisor: Great. Now assets—this is the “what’s working for you” section. Roughly how much do you consider investable across accounts, excluding the house?
Client 1: Total investable is 620850.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s break that into the main accounts so I understand where it sits. Do you have a joint taxable account?
Client 1: Yes—joint taxable. That one is 283650, held on a retirement platform.

Client 1: We’ve got a joint bank-type account on that same retirement platform—47600.
Advisor: Great. And then the home—just so we capture the full picture—what are you valuing the primary residence at?
Client 2: We’ve been using 201350 for the house value.
Advisor: Perfect. Last category I want to make sure we don’t miss: protection. David, do you have disability coverage in place through a policy you pay for directly?
Client 1: Yes. Disability policy—monthly premium is 950, coverage amount is 1.40M, and it’s covered until 2050-04-09.

Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high level with income, then expenses, then what you’ve got saved, and we’ll hit any insurance and debts along the way. David, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, then we’ll tighten it up.

Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—David, your self-employed income is part of that, and Jennifer, you’ve got income coming in too, right?

Client 1: The number we’ve been using is 181100 annually—household gross.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—David, your self-employed income is part of that, and Jennifer, you’ve got income coming in too, right?

Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high level with income, then expenses, then what you’ve got saved, and we’ll hit any insurance and debts along the way. David, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, then we’ll tighten it up.
Client 1: Roughly one-eighty a year, give or take.
Advisor: Okay—when you say one-eighty, do you mean around $180,000, or do you have a more exact figure you use for planning?
Client 1: The number we’ve been using is 181100 annually—household gross.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—David, your self-employed income is part of that, and Jennifer, you’ve got income coming in too, right?

Client 2: Yes—mortgage balance outstanding is 0, and we don’t have any other debt.
Client 1: Yep.
Advisor: Okay—income and cash flow. Before we get into the different income streams, just a couple household basics so I don’t assume wrong. David, Jennifer—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And it’s just the two of you at home—two adults in the household?

Advisor: Hi David, hi Jennifer—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks, Alex. I’m David. Win for me is getting a clear retirement timeline and making sure cash flow stays stable even if my business income bounces around.
Client 2: And I’m Jennifer. I just want to feel like it’s predictable month to month—like we’re not guessing.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high level with income, then expenses, then what you’ve got saved, and we’ll hit any insurance and debts along the way. David, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, then we’ll tighten it up.
Client 1: Roughly one-eighty a year, give or take.

Advisor: Got it. And it’s just the two of you at home—two adults in the household?
Client 2: Yes—2 adults.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one’s more of a planning input: how would you describe your overall risk tolerance—conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits best?
Client 1: Aggressive.
Advisor: Okay. And Jennifer, just to make sure I’m not missing any state-specific stuff—what state do you consider your primary residence for planning purposes?

Advisor: Got it. And on the house—any mortgage balance still outstanding, or is it paid off?
Client 2: It’s paid off—mortgage balance outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Okay. Any other debt floating around—credit cards you carry, car note, student loans—anything like that?
Client 1: No, non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Understood—on the credit cards, are you paying them in full each month, or mostly not using them?

Advisor: Thanks—super helpful. I want to grab a couple planning inputs that affect taxes and paperwork, then we’ll keep moving.
Advisor: David, Jennifer—when you think about your marginal tax bracket right now, what bracket are you usually planning around?
Client 1: We plan around 32%.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm the country for planning and assumptions—are we talking US?
Client 2: Yes, US.

Client 2: Yes, monthly. Annual amount is 44750.
Advisor: Great—thank you. Let me just pause one second and note that down.
Advisor: Okay. On the assets we covered—just to keep it clean in my notes—there’s the joint taxable account valued at 283650 on a retirement platform, David’s 401(k)/IRA bucket at a brokerage valued at 203500, the joint bank-type account at 47600 on that retirement platform, Jennifer’s private markets account on an advisor platform at 86150, and the primary residence valued at 201350. Did I miss anything material?
Client 1: No, that’s the full list.
Client 2: That’s everything we talked about, yeah.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Jennifer—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks, Alex. I’m David. Win for me is getting a clear retirement timeline and making sure cash flow stays stable even if my business income bounces around.
Client 2: And I’m Jennifer. I just want to feel like it’s predictable month to month—like we’re not guessing.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high level with income, then expenses, then what you’ve got saved, and we’ll hit any insurance and debts along the way. David, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, then we’ll tighten it up.

Client 1: Roughly one-eighty a year, give or take.

Client 1: The number we’ve been using is 181100 annually—household gross.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—David, your self-employed income is part of that, and Jennifer, you’ve got income coming in too, right?

Client 1: Yes, my household role is primary.
Advisor: Great—appreciate it. David, switching to your work income, what gross annual income number do you use from the business?
Client 1: Roughly one-twenty-seven a year—hang on—gross annual income is 127250.
Advisor: Thank you. Jennifer, on your side, your pension is separate from David’s business income—correct?
Client 2: Yes, my pension is separate.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Jennifer—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks, Alex. I’m David. Win for me is getting a clear retirement timeline and making sure cash flow stays stable even if my business income bounces around.
Client 2: And I’m Jennifer. I just want to feel like it’s predictable month to month—like we’re not guessing.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high level with income, then expenses, then what you’ve got saved, and we’ll hit any insurance and debts along the way. David, what’s the household gross income per year right now—ballpark is fine, then we’ll tighten it up.

Advisor: And any meaningful cash or bank savings you think of as investable—joint, maybe?
Client 1: We’ve got a joint bank-type account on that same retirement platform—47600.
Advisor: Great. And then the home—just so we capture the full picture—what are you valuing the primary residence at?
Client 2: We’ve been using 201350 for the house value.
Advisor: Perfect. Last category I want to make sure we don’t miss: protection. David, do you have disability coverage in place through a policy you pay for directly?

Advisor: Thank you. Jennifer, on your side, your pension is separate from David’s business income—correct?
Client 2: Yes, my pension is separate.
Advisor: Got it. David, what broad line of work would you put yourself in—operations, tech, sales, finance—what’s the closest fit?
Client 1: Sales.
Advisor: Makes sense. And day-to-day, is it mostly client relationships and driving revenue?

Advisor: Okay—when you say one-eighty, do you mean around $180,000, or do you have a more exact figure you use for planning?
Client 1: The number we’ve been using is 181100 annually—household gross.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—David, your self-employed income is part of that, and Jennifer, you’ve got income coming in too, right?
Client 2: Yes. I’m not working now, but I have a pension. David’s is the self-employed piece.
Advisor: Helpful. David, can you walk me through the main income streams—like rental, dividends, business—just the big buckets?

Client 2: Yes—spouse/partner.
Advisor: Perfect. And income-wise for you—separate from the pension we already noted—what gross annual income number should I use for you overall?
Client 2: It’s basically the pension—gross annual income is 53900.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. David, quick confirm on your side: you’re still self employed, right?
Client 1: Yes, employment status is self employed.

Advisor: That makes sense. Before we build the “predictable” version, I want to make sure I’ve got your work situation right, Jennifer—are you working right now or not currently working?
Client 2: Not working right now—my employment status is inactive.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And just so I describe your role correctly in the plan—Jennifer, you’re David’s spouse/partner, right?
Client 2: Yes—spouse/partner.
Advisor: Perfect. And income-wise for you—separate from the pension we already noted—what gross annual income number should I use for you overall?

Client 2: Steady, honestly. I’m fine with some travel, but I want it to feel predictable.
Advisor: That makes sense. Before we build the “predictable” version, I want to make sure I’ve got your work situation right, Jennifer—are you working right now or not currently working?
Client 2: Not working right now—my employment status is inactive.

Client 2: And I’m Jennifer. I just want to feel like it’s predictable month to month—like we’re not guessing.
